title: Improving Water Table Kinematic Conditions with Unsaturated Flow Insights

research.question: Can a water-table kinematic condition inherit unsaturated-flow physics while remaining analytically tractable and avoiding empirical fitting parameters?
research.system: A radially symmetric anisotropic unconfined aquifer pumped by a partially penetrating well beneath a thin unsaturated zone.
research.inputData: Coupled saturated-unsaturated flow equations, Gardner unsaturated hydraulic functions, pumping geometry, and drawdown records from BHRS wells B2, B4, B5, and B6 in Boise, Idaho.
research.method: Collapse a thin unsaturated layer into a water-table boundary containing gravity-drainage and radial-Laplacian terms, derive a transformed drawdown solution, and estimate aquifer parameters with regularized bounded least squares.
research.benchmark: The full coupled saturated-unsaturated model, the Neuman (1972) kinematic condition, a proposed-condition variant without the radial Laplacian, and BHRS field drawdown.
research.uncertainty: SEE and ME quantify residual fit; parameter bounds and regularization stabilize inversion; truncation error and field L/b ratios define the thin-zone applicability boundary.
research.conclusion: The radial-Laplacian boundary better reproduces coupled-model behavior and yields plausible specific yield without extra empirical parameters, provided the unsaturated zone is thin, rapidly draining, and has negligible upper flux.

narrative.throughline: Compressing the unsaturated zone into the water table need not erase its physics if storage and radial flow survive as visible boundary terms.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience knows that pumping drawdown in an unconfined aquifer receives delayed gravity drainage from above the water table.
narrative.stakes: An instantaneous-drainage boundary can underestimate specific yield and therefore mischaracterize available groundwater.
narrative.resolution: The compressed boundary reconnects Boise drawdown fits to unsaturated-zone flow while exposing the thin-zone limit that controls interpretation.

background.premise: The classical Neuman water-table condition can underestimate specific yield because it treats gravity drainage as instantaneous.
background.visibleEvidence: An S-shaped drawdown curve beside an instantaneous recharge pulse and a smaller inferred storage bracket.
background.whyNow: This is the discrepancy the new boundary must repair.
background.boundary: The paper targets analytical pumping-test interpretation in unconfined aquifers.
background.sourceExcerpt: widely used water table condition This is an open access article under the proposed by Neuman (1972), derived from the free surface equation, is known to often yield lower specific yield terms of the Creative Commons estimates than laboratory‐derived values, as noted by Nwankwor et al. (1992) and Malama (2011). Nwankwor Attribution License, which permits use, et al. (1992) attributed this discrepancy to the implicit assumption in Neuman (1972) water table condition that distribution and reproduction in any gravity drainage occurs instantaneously with water level decline.
background.visualObject: Unconfined-aquifer drawdown curve with instantaneous versus delayed drainage
background.motionPurpose: Contrast a sudden recharge pulse with the measured S-shaped response.
background.stepDetail: Start pumping; lower the water table; release water instantly in the classical case; reveal the delayed field response.
background.transitionBridge: The mismatch opens the unsaturated layer above the water table.

background.premise: Many improved kinematic conditions fit drawdown with parameters whose hydrophysical meaning is unclear.
background.visibleEvidence: Several fitted response curves converge while their unlabeled empirical knobs remain different.
background.whyNow: The proposed condition must improve fit without introducing another opaque parameter.
background.boundary: This criticism applies to fitting parameters rather than all parameters in prior models.
background.sourceExcerpt: However, the hydrophysical implications of these parameters is frequently unclear.
background.visualObject: Matched drawdown curves attached to ambiguous empirical controls
background.motionPurpose: Detach the ambiguous controls and replace them with measurable unsaturated properties.
background.stepDetail: Align prior curves; expose their fitting controls; fade the uncertain meanings; retain the physical layer.
background.transitionBridge: The physical unsaturated layer becomes the source of the new boundary terms.

background.premise: Letting unsaturated-zone thickness L approach zero reduces the unsaturated zone to a water-table boundary condition.
background.visibleEvidence: An unsaturated-zone thickness bracket L contracts toward the water table until only a boundary marker remains.
background.whyNow: The reduction is the model's central approximation and final claim boundary.
background.boundary: This premise owns the thin-layer reduction only; M20 separately tests the simultaneous applicability conditions.
background.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.2, paragraph preceding Equation (10)
background.sourceExcerpt: Therefore, the unsaturated zone can be reduced to a boundary condition by letting L → 0 (see Figure 2).
background.visualObject: Unsaturated layer compressed onto the water-table interface
background.motionPurpose: Collapse thickness while preserving storage and horizontal-flow traces at the interface.
background.stepDetail: Mark thickness L; shrink L toward zero; retain the water-table interface as the resulting boundary.
background.transitionBridge: Before the thin-layer reduction, M01 establishes the finite saturated and unsaturated governing equations.

methods.input: Saturated aquifer around a partially penetrating pumping well
methods.output: Saturated drawdown Equation (1)
methods.validity: Section 2.1 introduces Equation (1) as the mathematical model for aquifer drawdown before the boundary conditions are applied.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The mathematical model characterizing the aquifer drawdown in the unsaturated‐saturated flow system can be expressed as:
methods.visualObject: Saturated drawdown Equation (1) beside the radial aquifer and partially penetrating well
methods.motionPurpose: Attach the source-stated aquifer drawdown equation to the saturated domain.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the partially penetrating well geometry and place Equation (1) only in the saturated aquifer.
methods.transitionBridge: The finite system next receives its explicitly stated no-flow exterior conditions.

methods.input: Finite saturated-unsaturated aquifer domain
methods.output: No-infiltration condition at the unsaturated top and impermeable condition at the aquifer base
methods.validity: The source states no-flow boundaries at the top of the unsaturated zone and at the impermeable aquifer base.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1, physical boundary-condition paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: Boundary conditions are defined to reflect the physical limits of the system: a no‐flow boundary is set at the top of the unsaturated zone, signifying no infiltration, and at the base of the aquifer, where an impermeable layer blocks vertical flow.
methods.visualObject: No-flow markers at the unsaturated-zone top and impermeable aquifer base
methods.motionPurpose: Close the two source-stated exterior boundaries before interface coupling.
methods.stepDetail: Place a no-infiltration marker at the unsaturated top and an impermeable no-flow marker at the aquifer base.
methods.transitionBridge: The externally closed layers still require continuity where they meet at the water table.

methods.input: Saturated and unsaturated zones meeting at water-table point A
methods.output: Continuity condition at interface point A
methods.validity: Figure 2(a) identifies point A as the continuity location between the finite unsaturated and saturated zones.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1, Figure 2(a), interface at point A
methods.sourceExcerpt: Figure 2. Illustration of the unsaturated and saturated zones in relation to the water table. (a) Finite unsaturated zone with no‐ flow condition at point B and continuity at point A,
methods.visualObject: Saturated and unsaturated zones joined by a single continuity marker at water-table point A
methods.motionPurpose: Make point A visibly continuous before the unsaturated zone is collapsed.
methods.stepDetail: Bring the two zones to point A and replace their gap with one continuity marker, without adding an uncited flux construction.
methods.transitionBridge: The fully specified finite unsaturated field is now reduced by the first-order operation in M04.

methods.input: Finite unsaturated-zone flow equation with thickness L
methods.output: Unsaturated flow equation evaluated as a water-table boundary
methods.validity: The stated truncation error O(L/b) is accepted below 0.1.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Using a first‐order approximation, the truncation error O(L/ b) is acceptable if it remains below 0.1.
methods.visualObject: Two vertical derivative samples collapsing into one interface difference quotient
methods.motionPurpose: Bring the top and bottom samples together to show how the layer becomes a boundary.
methods.stepDetail: Mark values at z=b and z=b+L; form their difference; divide by L; collapse onto z=b.
methods.transitionBridge: The reduced equation receives physical constitutive properties in M05.

methods.input: Moisture-capacity function C0 at the reduced interface
methods.output: Gardner moisture-capacity expression C0 from Equation 13a
methods.validity: Equation (13a) gives the Gardner moisture-capacity expression used in the boundary derivation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: As outlined by Mishra and Neuman (2010), C0(z) and k0(z) can be expressed using the model proposed by Gardner (1958) as follows: C0(z) = Syaceak(b z), (13a)
methods.visualObject: Moisture-capacity placeholder C0 replaced by its Gardner expression
methods.motionPurpose: Show the moisture-capacity substitution as one constitutive operation.
methods.stepDetail: Isolate C0 in the reduced boundary; replace it with the Equation 13a expression.
methods.transitionBridge: The remaining conductivity placeholder receives its Gardner expression in M06.

methods.input: Relative-conductivity function k0 at the reduced interface
methods.output: Gardner relative-conductivity expression k0 from Equation 13b
methods.validity: Equation (13b) gives the Gardner relative-conductivity expression used in the boundary derivation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: and k0(z) = eak(φa φk+b z). (13b) Here, φa [L] indicates the air entry pressure and φk [L] is the pressure head above which relative hydraulic conductivity approaches unity. The Gardner exponent parameters ac [L 1] and ak [L 1] are related to water content and relative hydraulic conductivity, respectively.
methods.visualObject: Relative-conductivity placeholder k0 replaced by its Gardner expression
methods.motionPurpose: Show the conductivity substitution independently from moisture capacity.
methods.stepDetail: Isolate k0 in the reduced boundary; replace it with the Equation 13b expression.
methods.transitionBridge: The two constitutive substitutions meet the saturated drawdown at the water table in M07.

methods.input: Reduced boundary with Gardner C0 and k0 plus saturated and unsaturated drawdown fields
methods.output: Equation (14) rewritten under water-table drawdown continuity
methods.validity: The source uses saturated-unsaturated drawdown continuity at the water table to rewrite Equation (12) as Equation (14).
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2, continuity statement and Equation (14)
methods.sourceExcerpt: At the water table, the saturated and unsaturated drawdowns are continuous (i.e., s = σ); thus, Equation 12 can be rewritten as
methods.visualObject: Saturated and unsaturated drawdown traces meeting at the water-table interface
methods.motionPurpose: Use continuity as the sole operation that closes the constitutive boundary.
methods.stepDetail: Bring the two drawdown traces to the same interface value; reveal Equation 14.
methods.transitionBridge: The completed boundary closes the aquifer problem transformed in M08.

methods.input: Dimensionless aquifer initial-boundary equations in time
methods.operation: Transform temporal equations into Laplace domain
methods.output: Laplace-domain initial-boundary system with parameter p
methods.validity: The Laplace transform eliminates the time variable from the initial-boundary system.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Applying the Laplace transform to eliminate the time variable, the above equations can be transformed as.
methods.visualObject: Time axis collapsing into the Laplace parameter p beside the aquifer equations
methods.motionPurpose: Replace temporal evolution with a parameterized boundary-value system.
methods.stepDetail: Start from Equations A1a-A1f; map time derivatives to p; retain the radial and vertical coordinates; display Equations A2a-A2e.
methods.transitionBridge: The Laplace-domain system supplies the confined correction in M09.

methods.input: Laplace-domain system for the partially penetrating pumping well
methods.operation: Transform confined correction with finite Fourier cosine modes
methods.output: Laplace-domain drawdown correction s1,D for confined flow
methods.validity: The paper applies the finite Fourier cosine transform to the partially penetrating correction.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The drawdown correction s1,D in the Laplace domain can be obtained by using the finite Fourier cosine transform (Mishra & Neuman, 2011):
methods.visualObject: Partially penetrating screen resolved into vertical cosine modes for s1,D
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the screen-depth correction into confined-flow modes.
methods.stepDetail: Isolate the partial-screen geometry; project it onto cosine modes; display the Laplace-domain correction in Equation A3.
methods.transitionBridge: The confined correction enters the water-table condition transformed in M10.

methods.input: Laplace-domain Equation A5 with radial coordinate rD
methods.operation: Transform radial coordinate into Hankel domain
methods.output: Laplace-Hankel-domain vertical equations A6-A10
methods.validity: The Laplace-Hankel transforms eliminate rD from Equation A5.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Equation A5 can be solved using Laplace‐Hankel transforms to eliminate rD as
methods.visualObject: Radial Bessel modes replacing the rD coordinate in the water-table condition
methods.motionPurpose: Convert radial spreading into independent Hankel modes.
methods.stepDetail: Show Equation A5 in radius; introduce the Hankel parameter; collapse radial derivatives into mode factors; retain the vertical coordinate zD.
methods.transitionBridge: The radial transform leaves the vertical boundary-value problem solved in M11.

methods.input: Transformed vertical boundary-value equations A6-A10
methods.output: Laplace-Hankel-domain vertical solution
methods.validity: The source states that the transformed-domain solution is obtained after Equations (A6)-(A10).
methods.locator: PDF page 16, Appendix A, Equations (A6)-(A10), solution immediately below Equation (A10)
methods.sourceExcerpt: The solution in the Laplace‐Hankel domain can then be obtained as
methods.visualObject: Transformed vertical equations resolving into one Laplace-Hankel-domain solution profile
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the transformed vertical problem into the source-stated domain solution.
methods.stepDetail: Hold Equations (A6)-(A10) beside the vertical coordinate; resolve them into the displayed transformed-domain solution.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved vertical modes return to radius in M12.

methods.input: Laplace-Hankel-domain solution in zD
methods.operation: Transform Hankel solution into Laplace domain
methods.output: Laplace-domain radial integral for s2,D
methods.validity: The Hankel inverse transform restores rD through the zero-order Bessel function J0.
methods.locator: PDF page 16, Appendix A, inverse-Hankel integral immediately after Equation (A10)
methods.sourceExcerpt: The solution in the Laplace domain can be obtained using Hankel inverse transform:
methods.visualObject: Hankel modes integrated into the radial unconfined response s2,D
methods.motionPurpose: Reassemble radial distance from the transformed modes.
methods.stepDetail: Weight each mode by J0; integrate over the Hankel parameter; reveal the radial Laplace-domain solution.
methods.transitionBridge: The radial Laplace-domain result is inverted in M13.

methods.input: Laplace-domain drawdown components
methods.output: Time-domain confined-flow correction and unconfined-flow response
methods.validity: The source approximates the time-domain result with the numerical Stehfest scheme.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The time‐domain result can be approximated using a numerical method for Laplace inversion, specifically the Stehfest (1970) scheme, with the related formula shown below:
methods.visualObject: Weighted Stehfest samples reconstructing each drawdown component over time
methods.motionPurpose: Recover temporal drawdown from discrete Laplace evaluations.
methods.stepDetail: Sample the Laplace solution at Stehfest abscissae; apply the listed coefficients; reveal each component on the time axis.
methods.transitionBridge: The time-domain components are added in M14.

methods.input: Time-domain confined flow correction s1,D and unconfined flow response s2,D
methods.output: Dimensionless drawdown sD
methods.validity: Equation (19) defines the dimensionless drawdown solution sD = s1,D + s2,D.
methods.locator: PDF page 7, Section 2.5, Equation (19) and following definition
methods.sourceExcerpt: The dimensionless drawdown solution can be obtained as, following the procedure outlined by Neuman (1974): sD = s1,D + s2,D
methods.visualObject: Confined correction and unconfined response curves summing into total drawdown
methods.motionPurpose: Combine the two physical response components into the forward drawdown curve.
methods.stepDetail: Align s1,D and s2,D on one time axis; add their ordinates; reveal total sD from Equation 19.
methods.transitionBridge: The computed response becomes the forward curve fitted in M15.

methods.input: Measured or synthetic drawdown and forward-model predictions
methods.output: Parameter estimates minimizing observed-predicted squared error
methods.validity: The source defines the fit as minimization of the sum of squared errors between observed and predicted values.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 2.6, Equation (22), least-squares paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: This method minimizes the sum of squared errors between observed and predicted values, making it well‐suited for hydrogeological modeling where model complexity and non‐linearity are prevalent (Aster et al., 2018; Tarantola, 2005).
methods.visualObject: Measured points and model curve with squared residual segments contracting under least squares
methods.motionPurpose: Contract the observed-predicted squared errors under the source-stated objective.
methods.stepDetail: Draw observations and predictions; square the residual segments; reduce their sum to fit the parameters.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted residuals supply the SEE calculation in M16.

methods.input: Squared residuals from the fitted drawdown model and degrees of freedom nu
methods.output: SEE as the square root of summed squared residuals divided by nu
methods.validity: Equation (23) defines SEE with the fitted residuals and degrees of freedom.
methods.sourceExcerpt: SEE is calculated as the square root of the sum of squared residuals divided by the degrees of freedom
methods.visualObject: Squared residual segments accumulated under a square-root bracket
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the inversion residuals into SEE without combining another statistic.
methods.stepDetail: Sum the squared residuals; divide by nu; take the square root to reveal SEE.
methods.transitionBridge: The same fitted residuals supply the separate ME calculation in M17.

methods.input: Signed residuals from the fitted drawdown model
methods.output: ME as the arithmetic mean of the residuals
methods.validity: Equation (24) defines ME as the residual sum divided by the number of measured points.
methods.sourceExcerpt: ME is the mean of the residuals: LIN AND LIN 8 of 20 Water Resources Research 10.1029/2024WR038724 19447973, 2025, 3, Downloaded from https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1029/2024WR038724 by Chung Yuan Christian University, Wiley Online Library on [13/07/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License Figure 3. Temporal dimensionless drawdown distribution predicted by the proposed condition, the Neuman (1972) condition, and the coupled saturated‐unsaturated flow model (CSU flow model). The subplot (a) shows the results on a semi‐log scale with standard dimensions, while (b) presents a zoomed‐in view on the same semi‐log scale. ME = 1M (24)
methods.visualObject: Signed residual segments merging into one mean-error marker
methods.motionPurpose: Compute the signed residual bias independently from SEE.
methods.stepDetail: Sum the signed residuals; divide by the measured-point count; reveal ME.
methods.transitionBridge: The calibrated forward model is compared with alternative boundaries in M18.

methods.input: Proposed Neuman no-radial-Laplacian and full coupled-model drawdown responses
methods.output: Time-dependent evidence that the radial Laplacian recovers unsaturated radial recharge
methods.validity: All alternatives share the stated default geometry and parameters in Sections 3.1-3.4.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The curvature of the drawdown at late times is evidently influenced by the radial Laplacian, which enhances the prediction by allowing radial water flow in the unsaturated zone, closely approximating the coupled saturated‐unsaturated flow model.
methods.visualObject: Drawdown curves and streamlines with the radial-Laplacian contribution toggled
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal the horizontal unsaturated recharge path that bends the late-time curve toward the coupled model.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay curves; remove the radial term; compare late-time curvature; restore it; reveal radial streamlines.
methods.transitionBridge: The physically favored condition is taken to Boise observations in M19.

methods.input: BHRS drawdown records from wells B2 B4 B5 and B6
methods.output: Site-specific hydraulic estimates with predicted drawdowns against measured data
methods.validity: Most observations are represented well; the paper identifies neglected early-time wellbore storage as the main mismatch.
methods.locator: PDF pages 12-13, Section 3.5, Table 2 and Figure 8
methods.sourceExcerpt: The predicted drawdowns align closely with the measured data, except at early times, where the wellbore storage effect is neglected in the model.
methods.visualObject: Four BHRS observation-well curves with early-time mismatch highlighted
methods.motionPurpose: Attach the fitted boundary response to each measured well while preserving the early-time exception.
methods.stepDetail: Place four data traces; reveal fitted curves; mark early-time departures; show inferred specific-yield range.
methods.transitionBridge: The field geometry supplies an L/b ratio for the applicability test in M20.

methods.input: Unsaturated zone thickness L/ b, rapid drainage characteristics, and upper-boundary flux
methods.output: Explicit domain for reliable use of the reduced boundary
methods.validity: Section 3.6 states the simultaneous conditions and documents field ratios at BHRS Borden and Cape Cod.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Our comprehensive analysis reveals that optimal model performance requires several key conditions to be met simultaneously: the unsaturated zone thickness should maintain L/ b < 0.1 to ensure minimal truncation errors, the soil matrix should exhibit rapid drainage characteristics typical in sandy soils, and the upper boundary should demonstrate negligible flux conditions.
methods.visualObject: Applicability window with L/b thickness gauge drainage arrows and zero-flux top
methods.motionPurpose: Close the validity window unless all three physical conditions remain satisfied.
methods.stepDetail: Test L/b; test drainage speed; test upper flux; retain only the simultaneous valid region.
methods.transitionBridge: The valid window returns to the original pumped aquifer and field interpretation.

symbols.transition: Transform two thickness brackets into a ratio

symbols.visualObject: Water-volume bracket released per water-table decline
symbols.transition: Convert released pore volume into a storage label

symbols.visualObject: Radial spreading of drawdown along the water table
symbols.meaning: Radial component of the cylindrical Laplacian
symbols.transition: Transform radial flow arrows into the boundary term

return.visualObject: BHRS pumped-aquifer section with fitted drawdown curves and the L/b applicability gauge
return.claim: The physically parameterized boundary improves specific-yield interpretation and tracks the coupled saturated-unsaturated response without additional empirical fitting parameters.
return.claimBoundary: The result is conditioned on a thin unsaturated zone, rapid drainage, and negligible upper flux; early-time BHRS mismatch reflects neglected wellbore storage, and thicker zones require further development.
return.sourceExcerpt: Specifically, the model operates under the assumption of a thin unsaturated zone (L/b < 0.1).

visual.primaryObject: Partially penetrating well in an unconfined aquifer beneath a thin unsaturated zone
visual.accentObject: Unsaturated layer compressed into a radial-flow water-table boundary
visual.measuredQuantity: Pumping-induced drawdown and inferred specific yield
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the radial cross-section continuous from two-layer flow to the collapsed interface. Use horizontal arrows for the radial Laplacian, vertical pore-release motion for drainage, and preserve the L/b gauge through the field-return frame.
